author: @makers_lab
text: Currently using a GF Pro, but I like that the top Gweike has an RF rather than DC laser (looks like yours is the RF one as on their site that's the only model with the red button, though there image inconsistencies so it's uncertain and there might not be a water cooler for the RF laser), which should give a range of benefits such as better beam quality. The GF does have two cameras as you said, and uses one for focussing that's built into the head assembly along with a red laser, and the one in the lid similar to the Gweike. Would have been good to have seen a comparison with their RF laser (if it's that model) to whatever DC lasers you have as there should be reduced kerf and less smoke for example.

author: @Alex_Unlocked
text: When you get sent these to review are you allowed to keep them or do you have to give them back ?

author: @sharkur
text:
good review, but i found one mistake. 
you can feed material endlessly from front to back. you have to remove the ruber seal at the back and open the front.
i read online there is even a second way for endless feeding, somehow from below the machine.

bonus: the extra camera wire is for a direct connection to your pc, for lightburn support.
btw gweikes support is very good. used it already twice.

author: @CocoBorderCollie
text: Great video, thank you so much

author: @Berkana
text: With a large laser cutter, you could cut the base plate template for a Ugrinsky turbine, and perhaps make some built-up forms from laser cut sheets for bending the turbine walls. Are you going to cover the Ugrinsky turbine at some point?

author: @neilrisley6667
text: Do you know if it will work with LaserGRBL?

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: Dr. Smith, you may have covered this already, how about a lecture on file format conversions. Laser scribe machine require a specific file format. This really can be boiled down to which formats the cutter can process and which programs out there will give you that desired format from the original image. I'm with everyone else. If I buy it, it's mine, stay out of my business, unless it breaks. Example, I use GIMP to path SVGs for use in FreeCAD. Free conversion, and I don't pay for my own artwork creations.

author: @NZHippie
text: That big white start button should be really (from a safety perspective & ergonomic prospective) the emergency stop button and the hard to reach small red button should be the start button...

author: @athreedognight4022
text: The adds I've seen for around the last 2 to 3 months is the new X-Tool diode lasers, for about $1000 without the case, that's without the roller also for the bottles, I think that's close?

replies.author: @sharkur
replies.text: diode and co2 laser are very different. e.g. only co2 can cut propperly acrylic glas. also the x tool has just 20w, the gweike 50w...

author: @Twistedmetal-qe8kx
text: I agree, the online only is a bit of a deal breaker for me. Looks like an impressive machine, thanks for the interesting look inside.

author: @scotttovey
text:
Glowforge charging 6 grand for their laser but then requiring an online presence is like someone selling you a house and not moving out.

There is absolutely no way any nation on earth, should allow this to be legal.

It's a bit sad if you think about it.

The Communist are showing Capitalists through their actions, what constitute a legal and proper sale because the capitalists have gotten so greedy, that they want to claim to have sold you something, all the while retaining ownership of it.

It makes me want to load up my coconut cream pie filling cannon and start firing away.

It's a good thing I haven't actually built the thing.
Can you imagine how annoyed the three letter agency peeps would be, standing their with their suits all coconut cream pied?

I don't think they would get the joke, do you?
😂

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - no I don't think they would lol - by the way your bit - it's like selling the house and not moving out - awesome way of putting it mate

author: @hubrisnaut
text: Considering Robert's current interests in 3d printing and lasers, I'd like to point to a technology I envisioned as a precocious child (I predicted at 12, in 1975;  the Milky Way had a black hole at it's center.). Electromagnetic (inc. photons) standing wave or interference pattern atomic deposition. The tech is now coming to fruition in the lab with previously unattained 3d printed crystalline structures.

replies.author: @hubrisnaut
replies.text: A 2d form of the concept might be the commercially available example of ultraviolet light activated adhesives.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is very interesting mate - I must look into it because you are right it is an interest of mine

author: @actudoran
text: what kind of materials and how thick can be cut with each machine? .... thing is ... I would pay top bucks for something that can cut even 2 mm of steel sheets... open up a whole lot of options to a home workshop

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you need about a 500W laser to do that mate - Geike is 50W and Glowforge is 45W - so no chance of doing that they will do 12-13mm of wood

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
Giving another watch to make up for it and a comment because I didn't have the time to earlier.

I'm 100% with you on the offline or stand-alone operation. That's exactly why I backed out (same goes for any physical tool requiring subscription...like they said "You will own nothing and you will be happy") when 10 friends were wanting to go in on a Glowforge. 
Either I buy the thing that does a thing and it's mine too use to do the thing or you can go _insert_ _colorful-metaphor_ _here_.
